score_basis: score_lab_v2_5_3:required_weighted_geometric_mean(proof 1.0x, edge 0.9x, ride 1.7x, stability 1.0x, viability 1.35x); missing mandatory axes score as zero

cost_model.assumption: Costs are applied as a round-trip percent drag to resolved trade outcomes before matrix, path, and Score Lab metrics are computed.

components.detail: Probability-style confidence that the edge is not just noise. In normal deep replay this comes from PSR; when a sweep supplies a multiple-testing-aware override it can represent DSR. If PSR cannot be computed, Score Lab treats belief as zero rather than silently dropping the proof gate.
components.formula: PSR/DSR belief banded from 0.55 to 0.98; missing belief = 0
components.label: PSR / DSR belief

components.detail: More resolved trades make the proof more credible. This does not say the strategy is profitable by itself; it says the evidence base is less thin.
components.label: Trade support

axes.detail: Statistical and sample-confidence evidence. This axis answers whether the observed edge deserves belief before we get excited about the size of the return.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: belief 1.5x, trade support 1.0x
axes.label: Proof

components.detail: Average net R earned per resolved trade. This is the most direct economic edge input and should rise when each trade is worth more.
components.label: Expectancy

components.detail: Gross win/loss efficiency above breakeven. It complements expectancy by checking whether gains are not merely a small edge on huge turnover. When profit factor is undefined, this component stays unavailable and Proof must carry the sample-quality concern.
components.label: Profit factor

axes.detail: Economic power of the strategy when it fires. This axis is about whether the strategy has useful return mechanics, separate from whether the path is comfortable or the pocket is stable.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: expectancy 1.2x, profit factor 1.0x
axes.label: Edge

components.detail: Rewards shallower maximum drawdown in R. This is the headline capital-pain input: lower drawdown produces a higher normalized value.
components.label: Drawdown resilience

components.detail: Captures persistent drawdown pressure, not just the single worst trough. It is useful when two curves have similar max drawdown but one spends much longer wounded.
components.label: Ulcer resilience

components.detail: Penalizes time spent below prior equity highs. This is about psychological and capital lockup cost across the run.
components.label: Underwater resilience

components.detail: Aggregates the capital and patience cost of holding the strategy through the run: worst drawdown, persistent drawdown pressure, and time spent underwater.
components.label: Capital pain

components.detail: Penalizes strategies that make the trader sit through long runs of stopped-out trades. This is intentionally more generic than an anti-12R rule: high-payoff systems can still pass if their realized trade sequence does not produce brutal loss streaks.
components.label: Loss-streak resilience

components.detail: Combines curve fit and K-ratio style trend strength. It rewards equity paths that accumulate instead of arriving through jagged endpoint luck.
components.label: Smoothness

components.detail: Converts the isolated spike penalty into a positive score. It tries to detect cells that look great mostly because one point outperforms unsupported neighbors.
components.label: Spike resistance

components.detail: Aggregates whether gains arrived in a steady, repeatable way: smooth curve shape and resistance to one-cell spike artifacts. Time concentration is scored separately as Gain spread so it has enough weight to matter.
components.label: Accrual quality

components.detail: Penalizes strategies whose equity result depends on a small number of positive days. This is the explicit concentration guard: a strategy can have a smooth-looking endpoint, but if most of the gain came from a few sessions, Ride should not read as comfortable.
components.formula: geomean(top-day concentration resistance, growth(effective positive days/weeks), positive-week participation)
components.label: Gain spread

axes.detail: Survivability and concentration of the equity path. V2 separates this into capital pain, loss-streak burden, and accrual quality so one pretty curve cannot hide a brutal realized trade sequence.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: capital pain 1.25x, loss streak 1.75x, accrual quality 1.0x, gain spread 2.4x
axes.label: Ride

components.detail: Share of searched TP/SL cells with positive expectancy. Wider profitable area means the strategy is less dependent on one exact exit policy.
components.label: Positive cell area

components.detail: Size of the largest connected profitable region. This is stronger than raw positive area because it rewards contiguous pockets over scattered hits.
components.label: Largest cluster

components.detail: Aggregates matrix-wide support: how much of the searched lattice is profitable and whether that profitable area forms a connected region.
components.label: Global pocket

components.detail: How well nearby cells support the best cell. A high value means the selected outcome is backed by its local neighborhood.
components.formula: mean of neighbor count, neighborhood count, and neighbor expectancy transforms
components.label: Neighbor support

components.detail: Checks whether small TP/SL perturbations keep similar economics. This helps separate stable pockets from isolated peaks.
components.formula: mean of expectancy-gap, cluster, and instrument-consensus support
components.label: Perturbation stability

components.detail: Direct count of positive neighbors around the best cell. It is intentionally simple and easy to audit.
components.label: Best-cell neighbors

components.detail: Aggregates neighborhood evidence around the chosen cell: adjacent support, perturbation stability, and direct positive neighbor count.
components.label: Local pocket

components.detail: Economic strength of the backend robust anchor. This keeps the stability axis from rewarding a large but weak green area too much.
components.label: Robust-cell expectancy

components.detail: Share of sane 1R-4R exit cells with positive expectancy. This rewards strategies whose edge survives in ordinary risk-reward space instead of only at the far right of the matrix.
components.label: Normal-R support

components.detail: Best expectancy available inside the 1R-4R band. High-R profiles are allowed to win, but they score better when a usable normal-R version of the edge also exists.
components.label: Normal-R edge

components.detail: Categorical matrix-shape readout. Broad high-R ridges stay healthy; thin tails and isolated high-R peaks receive low but nonzero stability credit.
components.formula: label map: normal_r_calibrated 1.0, broad_ridge_high_r_anchor 0.9, mixed_exit_surface 0.62, isolated_high_r_peak 0.22, thin_tail 0.08
components.label: Ridge continuity

components.detail: Checks how far the selected best cell outruns the best normal-R cell. A large gap is not banned, but it needs other surface evidence to keep the stability score strong.
components.label: Tail-gap resilience

components.detail: Checks the selected stop row, plus adjacent stop rows, for positive 0.5R-3R net expectancy. This catches high-R cells that only work as tail capture while ordinary exits on the same geometry are weak or negative.
components.formula: geomean(same-stop positive ratio, same-stop best expectancy, nearby-stop positive ratio, nearby-stop best expectancy)
components.label: Same-stop low-R support

components.detail: Normal-R and high-R matrix-shape evidence. This rewards broad exit support and now specifically checks whether the selected stop row has viable lower-R exits instead of only a distant tail target.
components.formula: geomean(normal-R support, normal-R edge, ridge continuity, tail-gap resilience, focused low-R support when available, and high-R region support when relevant)
components.label: Exit surface shape

axes.detail: Parameter and exit-pocket stability. V2 separates broad matrix health, local pocket support, and exit-surface shape so a scattered or tail-only grid does not read the same as a contiguous, locally supported pocket.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: global pocket 1.0x, local pocket 1.25x, robust expectancy 1.0x, exit surface shape 0.85x when available
axes.label: Stability

components.detail: Trade opportunity rate over the effective market window. Score Lab treats cadence as an ideal band: too few trades are hard to trust, a moderate rate is fully usable, and extreme churn is penalized instead of rewarded.
components.formula: banded cadence: ramps to full credit at 2 trades/month, holds through 24, then decays through overtrading zones
components.label: Cadence

components.detail: Rewards longer effective backtest windows. A strong 3-month result is treated as promising but incomplete evidence; a comparable 36-month result receives full evidence-window credit.
components.label: Longevity

components.detail: Share of participating instruments that support the selected pocket. This only affects the score when at least two instruments participated, so single-instrument specialists are not penalized for being intentionally focused.
components.label: Instrument consensus

components.detail: Checks whether entry signals are sparse enough to behave like deliberate opportunities instead of bar-to-bar saturation. It is intentionally a signal hygiene input, separate from trade cadence, and only participates when signal behavior data exists.
components.label: Entry spacing hygiene

components.detail: Penalizes exits whose stop distance is so small that spread, slippage, and commission consume a large fraction of each R. Costs are still applied arithmetically in replay; this makes the strategy-shape risk visible to Score Lab.
components.label: Execution friction

components.detail: Penalizes strategies that keep trades open for too long. Long holds create swap exposure, weekend risk, unavailable margin, and a poor operator experience even when final realized R looks good.
components.formula: geomean(exp(-avg_holding_hours / 72), exp(-p90_holding_hours / 168), exp(-max_holding_hours / 720)); floored at 3% when present
components.label: Hold burden

components.detail: Penalizes profiles that spend long stretches inactive, flat, or unable to make new equity highs. This catches the visual failure mode where most of the chart is dead and the useful behavior appears only in a narrow regime.
components.formula: geomean(exp(-longest_no_new_high_days / 240), exp(-ending_no_new_high_days / 180), exp(-inactive window edges / 180), active_span_ratio band); floored at 3% when present
components.label: Dormancy

axes.detail: Operational and evidence-window sanity checks. This axis asks whether the profile looks usable as a repeatable trading object, not just impressive in a short or narrow test.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: cadence 1.0x, longevity 1.35x, consensus 1.0x when 2+ instruments, entry spacing 1.25x, execution friction 1.35x, hold burden 1.75x, dormancy 1.55x
axes.label: Viability

score_lab_payload.basis: canonical five-axis score lab v2.5.3 for ranking, sweeps, Explorer, and autoresearch
score_lab_payload.combiner: required_weighted_geometric_mean(proof 1.0x, edge 0.9x, ride 1.7x, stability 1.0x, viability 1.35x); missing mandatory axes score as zero

Kept as a diagnostic composite; its ingredients are folded in separately.
Legacy baseline superseded by Score Lab; not an input when raw replay fields are available.
Diagnostic productivity metric. Removed from v2.2 Edge because it overlaps with cadence and can reward churn.
Removed from v2 scoring; ride should expose the real failure mode without a direct anti-high-R rule.
Underlying gain-distribution raw evidence now represented by gain_spread_resilience.

Gross win/loss efficiency over breakeven.

Gain concentration across positive days and weeks.
Converted into spike resistance.
Positive area, cluster, robust-cell edge, and neighbors.
Neighbor support and perturbation stability.
Normal-R support, ridge shape, and high-R isolation resistance when available.
Same-stop and adjacent-stop 0.5R-3R support for the selected high-R cell.
Banded opportunity cadence; rewards a usable range while penalizing churn.
Longevity credit for longer backtest evidence windows.
How many participating instruments agree when 2+ instruments are present.
Signal clustering and bar-to-bar saturation check when behavior data exists.
Round-trip execution cost as a fraction of stop distance.
Open-trade duration, swap exposure, and capital lockup pressure.
Long no-new-high stretches and inactive window edges.

components.detail: Probability-style confidence that the edge is not just noise. components.detail: More resolved trades make the proof more credible. This does not say the strategy is profitable by itself; it says the evidence base is less thin.

axes.detail: Statistical and sample-confidence evidence. This axis answers whether the observed edge deserves belief before we get excited about the size of the return.

axes.label: Proof

components.detail: Average net R earned per resolved trade. This is the most direct economic edge input and should rise when each trade is worth more.
components.label: Expectancy

components.detail: Gross win/loss efficiency above breakeven. It complements expectancy by checking whether gains are not merely a small edge on huge turnover. When profit factor is undefined, this component stays unavailable and Proof must carry the sample-quality concern.
components.label: Profit factor

axes.detail: Economic power of the strategy when it fires. This axis is about whether the strategy has useful return mechanics, separate from whether the path is comfortable or the pocket is stable.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: expectancy 1.2x, profit factor 1.0x
axes.label: Edge

components.detail: Rewards shallower maximum drawdown in R. This is the headline capital-pain input: lower drawdown produces a higher normalized value.
components.label: Drawdown resilience

components.detail: Captures persistent drawdown pressure, not just the single worst trough. It is useful when two curves have similar max drawdown but one spends much longer wounded.
components.label: Ulcer resilience

components.detail: Penalizes time spent below prior equity highs. This is about psychological and capital lockup cost across the run.
components.label: Underwater resilience

components.detail: Aggregates the capital and patience cost of holding the strategy through the run: worst drawdown, persistent drawdown pressure, and time spent underwater.
components.label: Capital pain

components.detail: Penalizes strategies that make the trader sit through long runs of stopped-out trades. This is intentionally more generic than an anti-12R rule: high-payoff systems can still pass if their realized trade sequence does not produce brutal loss streaks.
components.label: Loss-streak resilience

components.detail: Combines curve fit and K-ratio style trend strength. It rewards equity paths that accumulate instead of arriving through jagged endpoint luck.
components.label: Smoothness

components.detail: Converts the isolated spike penalty into a positive score. It tries to detect cells that look great mostly because one point outperforms unsupported neighbors.
components.label: Spike resistance

components.detail: Aggregates whether gains arrived in a steady, repeatable way: smooth curve shape and resistance to one-cell spike artifacts. Time concentration is scored separately as Gain spread so it has enough weight to matter.
components.label: Accrual quality

components.detail: Penalizes strategies whose equity result depends on a small number of positive days. This is the explicit concentration guard: a strategy can have a smooth-looking endpoint, but if most of the gain came from a few sessions, Ride should not read as comfortable.
components.formula: geomean(top-day concentration resistance, growth(effective positive days/weeks), positive-week participation)
components.label: Gain spread

components.detail: How well nearby cells support the best cell. A high value means the selected outcome is backed by its local neighborhood.

components.detail: Checks whether small TP/SL perturbations keep similar economics. This helps separate stable pockets from isolated peaks.

components.detail: Direct count of positive neighbors around the best cell. It is intentionally simple and easy to audit.

components.detail: Aggregates neighborhood evidence around the chosen cell: adjacent support, perturbation stability, and direct positive neighbor count.

components.detail: Economic strength of the backend robust anchor. This keeps the stability axis from rewarding a large but weak green area too much.

components.detail: Share of sane 1R-4R exit cells with positive expectancy. This rewards strategies whose edge survives in ordinary risk-reward space instead of only at the far right of the matrix.

components.label: Ulcer resilience

components.detail: Penalizes time spent below prior equity highs. This is about psychological and capital lockup cost across the run.
components.label: Underwater resilience

components.detail: Aggregates the capital and patience cost of holding the strategy through the run: worst drawdown, persistent drawdown pressure, and time spent underwater.
components.label: Capital pain

components.detail: Penalizes strategies that make the trader sit through long runs of stopped-out trades. This is intentionally more generic than an anti-12R rule: high-payoff systems can still pass if their realized trade sequence does not produce brutal loss streaks.
components.label: Loss-streak resilience

components.detail: Combines curve fit and K-ratio style trend strength. It rewards equity paths that accumulate instead of arriving through jagged endpoint luck.
components.label: Smoothness

components.detail: Converts the isolated spike penalty into a positive score. It tries to detect cells that look great mostly because one point outperforms unsupported neighbors.
components.label: Spike resistance

components.detail: Aggregates whether gains arrived in a steady, repeatable way: smooth curve shape and resistance to one-cell spike artifacts. Time concentration is scored separately as Gain spread so it has enough weight to matter.
components.label: Accrual quality

components.detail: Penalizes strategies whose equity result depends on a small number of positive days. This is the explicit concentration guard: a strategy can have a smooth-looking endpoint, but if most of the gain came from a few sessions, Ride should not read as comfortable.
components.formula: geomean(top-day concentration resistance, growth(effective positive days/weeks), positive-week participation)
components.label: Gain spread

axes.detail: Survivability and concentration of the equity path. V2 separates this into capital pain, loss-streak burden, and accrual quality so one pretty curve cannot hide a brutal realized trade sequence.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: capital pain 1.25x, loss streak 1.75x, accrual quality 1.0x, gain spread 2.4x
axes.label: Ride

components.detail: Share of searched TP/SL cells with positive expectancy. Wider profitable area means the strategy is less dependent on one exact exit policy.
components.label: Positive cell area

components.detail: Size of the largest connected profitable region. This is stronger than raw positive area because it rewards contiguous pockets over scattered hits.
components.label: Largest cluster

components.detail: Aggregates matrix-wide support: how much of the searched lattice is profitable and whether that profitable area forms a connected region.
components.label: Global pocket

components.detail: How well nearby cells support the best cell. A high value means the selected outcome is backed by its local neighborhood.
components.formula: mean of neighbor count, neighborhood count, and neighbor expectancy transforms
components.label: Neighbor support

components.detail: Checks whether small TP/SL perturbations keep similar economics. This helps separate stable pockets from isolated peaks.
components.formula: mean of expectancy-gap, cluster, and instrument-consensus support
components.label: Perturbation stability

components.detail: Direct count of positive neighbors around the best cell. It is intentionally simple and easy to audit.
components.label: Best-cell neighbors

components.detail: Aggregates neighborhood evidence around the chosen cell: adjacent support, perturbation stability, and direct positive neighbor count.
components.label: Local pocket

components.detail: Economic strength of the backend robust anchor. This keeps the stability axis from rewarding a large but weak green area too much.
components.label: Robust-cell expectancy

components.detail: Share of sane 1R-4R exit cells with positive expectancy. This rewards strategies whose edge survives in ordinary risk-reward space instead of only at the far right of the matrix.
components.label: Normal-R support

components.detail: Best expectancy available inside the 1R-4R band. High-R profiles are allowed to win, but they score better when a usable normal-R version of the edge also exists.
components.label: Normal-R edge

components.detail: Categorical matrix-shape readout. Broad high-R ridges stay healthy; thin tails and isolated high-R peaks receive low but nonzero stability credit.
components.formula: label map: normal_r_calibrated 1.0, broad_ridge_high_r_anchor 0.9, mixed_exit_surface 0.62, isolated_high_r_peak 0.22, thin_tail 0.08
components.label: Ridge continuity

components.detail: Checks how far the selected best cell outruns the best normal-R cell. A large gap is not banned, but it needs other surface evidence to keep the stability score strong.
components.label: Tail-gap resilience

components.detail: Checks the selected stop row, plus adjacent stop rows, for positive 0.5R-3R net expectancy. This catches high-R cells that only work as tail capture while ordinary exits on the same geometry are weak or negative.
components.formula: geomean(same-stop positive ratio, same-stop best expectancy, nearby-stop positive ratio, nearby-stop best expectancy)
components.label: Same-stop low-R support

components.detail: Normal-R and high-R matrix-shape evidence. This rewards broad exit support and now specifically checks whether the selected stop row has viable lower-R exits instead of only a distant tail target.
components.formula: geomean(normal-R support, normal-R edge, ridge continuity, tail-gap resilience, focused low-R support when available, and high-R region support when relevant)
components.label: Exit surface shape

axes.detail: Parameter and exit-pocket stability. V2 separates broad matrix health, local pocket support, and exit-surface shape so a scattered or tail-only grid does not read the same as a contiguous, locally supported pocket.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: global pocket 1.0x, local pocket 1.25x, robust expectancy 1.0x, exit surface shape 0.85x when available
axes.label: Stability

components.detail: Trade opportunity rate over the effective market window. Score Lab treats cadence as an ideal band: too few trades are hard to trust, a moderate rate is fully usable, and extreme churn is penalized instead of rewarded.
components.formula: banded cadence: ramps to full credit at 2 trades/month, holds through 24, then decays through overtrading zones
components.label: Cadence

components.detail: Rewards longer effective backtest windows. A strong 3-month result is treated as promising but incomplete evidence; a comparable 36-month result receives full evidence-window credit.
components.label: Longevity

components.detail: Share of participating instruments that support the selected pocket. This only affects the score when at least two instruments participated, so single-instrument specialists are not penalized for being intentionally focused.
components.label: Instrument consensus

components.detail: Checks whether entry signals are sparse enough to behave like deliberate opportunities instead of bar-to-bar saturation. It is intentionally a signal hygiene input, separate from trade cadence, and only participates when signal behavior data exists.
components.label: Entry spacing hygiene

components.detail: Penalizes exits whose stop distance is so small that spread, slippage, and commission consume a large fraction of each R. Costs are still applied arithmetically in replay; this makes the strategy-shape risk visible to Score Lab.
components.label: Execution friction

components.detail: Penalizes strategies that keep trades open for too long. Long holds create swap exposure, weekend risk, unavailable margin, and a poor operator experience even when final realized R looks good.
components.formula: geomean(exp(-avg_holding_hours / 72), exp(-p90_holding_hours / 168), exp(-max_holding_hours / 720)); floored at 3% when present
components.label: Hold burden

components.detail: Penalizes profiles that spend long stretches inactive, flat, or unable to make new equity highs. This catches the visual failure mode where most of the chart is dead and the useful behavior appears only in a narrow regime.
components.formula: geomean(exp(-longest_no_new_high_days / 240), exp(-ending_no_new_high_days / 180), exp(-inactive window edges / 180), active_span_ratio band); floored at 3% when present
components.label: Dormancy

axes.detail: Operational and evidence-window sanity checks. This axis asks whether the profile looks usable as a repeatable trading object, not just impressive in a short or narrow test.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: cadence 1.0x, longevity 1.35x, consensus 1.0x when 2+ instruments, entry spacing 1.25x, execution friction 1.35x, hold burden 1.75x, dormancy 1.55x
axes.label: Viability

cost_model.assumption: Costs are applied as a round-trip percent drag to resolved trade outcomes before matrix, path, and Score Lab metrics are computed.

components.detail: Probability-style confidence that the edge is not just noise. In normal deep replay this comes from PSR; when a sweep supplies a multiple-testing-aware override it can represent DSR. If PSR cannot be computed, Score Lab treats belief as zero rather than silently dropping the proof gate.
components.formula: PSR/DSR belief banded from 0.55 to 0.98; missing belief = 0
components.label: PSR / DSR belief

components.detail: More resolved trades make the proof more credible. This does not say the strategy is profitable by itself; it says the evidence base is less thin.
components.label: Trade support

axes.detail: Statistical and sample-confidence evidence. This axis answers whether the observed edge deserves belief before we get excited about the size of the return.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: belief 1.5x, trade support 1.0x
axes.label: Proof

components.detail: Average net R earned per resolved trade. This is the most direct economic edge input and should rise when each trade is worth more.
components.label: Expectancy

components.detail: Gross win/loss efficiency above breakeven. It complements expectancy by checking whether gains are not merely a small edge on huge turnover. When profit factor is undefined, this component stays unavailable and Proof must carry the sample-quality concern.
components.label: Profit factor

axes.detail: Economic power of the strategy when it fires. This axis is about whether the strategy has useful return mechanics, separate from whether the path is comfortable or the pocket is stable.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: expectancy 1.2x, profit factor 1.0x
axes.label: Edge

components.detail: Rewards shallower maximum drawdown in R. This is the headline capital-pain input: lower drawdown produces a higher normalized value.
components.label: Drawdown resilience

components.detail: Captures persistent drawdown pressure, not just the single worst trough. It is useful when two curves have similar max drawdown but one spends much longer wounded.
components.label: Ulcer resilience

components.detail: Penalizes time spent below prior equity highs. This is about psychological and capital lockup cost across the run.
components.label: Underwater resilience

components.detail: Aggregates the capital and patience cost of holding the strategy through the run: worst drawdown, persistent drawdown pressure, and time spent underwater.
components.label: Capital pain

components.detail: Penalizes strategies that make the trader sit through long runs of stopped-out trades. This is intentionally more generic than an anti-12R rule: high-payoff systems can still pass if their realized trade sequence does not produce brutal loss streaks.
components.label: Loss-streak resilience

components.detail: Combines curve fit and K-ratio style trend strength. It rewards equity paths that accumulate instead of arriving through jagged endpoint luck.
components.label: Smoothness

components.detail: Converts the isolated spike penalty into a positive score. It tries to detect cells that look great mostly because one point outperforms unsupported neighbors.
components.label: Spike resistance

components.detail: Aggregates whether gains arrived in a steady, repeatable way: smooth curve shape and resistance to one-cell spike artifacts. Time concentration is scored separately as Gain spread so it has enough weight to matter.
components.label: Accrual quality

components.detail: Penalizes strategies whose equity result depends on a small number of positive days. This is the explicit concentration guard: a strategy can have a smooth-looking endpoint, but if most of the gain came from a few sessions, Ride should not read as comfortable.
components.formula: geomean(top-day concentration resistance, growth(effective positive days/weeks), positive-week participation)
components.label: Gain spread

axes.detail: Survivability and concentration of the equity path. V2 separates this into capital pain, loss-streak burden, and accrual quality so one pretty curve cannot hide a brutal realized trade sequence.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: capital pain 1.25x, loss streak 1.75x, accrual quality 1.0x, gain spread 2.4x
axes.label: Ride

components.detail: Share of searched TP/SL cells with positive expectancy. Wider profitable area means the strategy is less dependent on one exact exit policy.
components.label: Positive cell area

components.detail: Size of the largest connected profitable region. This is stronger than raw positive area because it rewards contiguous pockets over scattered hits.
components.label: Largest cluster

components.detail: Aggregates matrix-wide support: how much of the searched lattice is profitable and whether that profitable area forms a connected region.
components.label: Global pocket

components.detail: How well nearby cells support the best cell. A high value means the selected outcome is backed by its local neighborhood.
components.formula: mean of neighbor count, neighborhood count, and neighbor expectancy transforms
components.label: Neighbor support

components.detail: Checks whether small TP/SL perturbations keep similar economics. This helps separate stable pockets from isolated peaks.
components.formula: mean of expectancy-gap, cluster, and instrument-consensus support
components.label: Perturbation stability

components.detail: Direct count of positive neighbors around the best cell. It is intentionally simple and easy to audit.
components.label: Best-cell neighbors

components.detail: Aggregates neighborhood evidence around the chosen cell: adjacent support, perturbation stability, and direct positive neighbor count.
components.label: Local pocket

components.detail: Economic strength of the backend robust anchor. This keeps the stability axis from rewarding a large but weak green area too much.
components.label: Robust-cell expectancy

components.detail: Share of sane 1R-4R exit cells with positive expectancy. This rewards strategies whose edge survives in ordinary risk-reward space instead of only at the far right of the matrix.
components.label: Normal-R support

components.detail: Best expectancy available inside the 1R-4R band. High-R profiles are allowed to win, but they score better when a usable normal-R version of the edge also exists.
components.label: Normal-R edge

components.detail: Categorical matrix-shape readout. Broad high-R ridges stay healthy; thin tails and isolated high-R peaks receive low but nonzero stability credit.
components.formula: label map: normal_r_calibrated 1.0, broad_ridge_high_r_anchor 0.9, mixed_exit_surface 0.62, isolated_high_r_peak 0.22, thin_tail 0.08
components.label: Ridge continuity

components.detail: Checks how far the selected best cell outruns the best normal-R cell. A large gap is not banned, but it needs other surface evidence to keep the stability score strong.
components.label: Tail-gap resilience

components.detail: Checks the selected stop row, plus adjacent stop rows, for positive 0.5R-3R net expectancy. This catches high-R cells that only work as tail capture while ordinary exits on the same geometry are weak or negative.
components.formula: geomean(same-stop positive ratio, same-stop best expectancy, nearby-stop positive ratio, nearby-stop best expectancy)
components.label: Same-stop low-R support

components.detail: Normal-R and high-R matrix-shape evidence. This rewards broad exit support and now specifically checks whether the selected stop row has viable lower-R exits instead of only a distant tail target.
components.formula: geomean(normal-R support, normal-R edge, ridge continuity, tail-gap resilience, focused low-R support when available, and high-R region support when relevant)
components.label: Exit surface shape

axes.detail: Parameter and exit-pocket stability. V2 separates broad matrix health, local pocket support, and exit-surface shape so a scattered or tail-only grid does not read the same as a contiguous, locally supported pocket.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: global pocket 1.0x, local pocket 1.25x, robust expectancy 1.0x, exit surface shape 0.85x when available
axes.label: Stability

components.detail: Trade opportunity rate over the effective market window. Score Lab treats cadence as an ideal band: too few trades are hard to trust, a moderate rate is fully usable, and extreme churn is penalized instead of rewarded.
components.formula: banded cadence: ramps to full credit at 2 trades/month, holds through 24, then decays through overtrading zones
components.label: Cadence

components.detail: Rewards longer effective backtest windows. A strong 3-month result is treated as promising but incomplete evidence; a comparable 36-month result receives full evidence-window credit.
components.label: Longevity

components.detail: Share of participating instruments that support the selected pocket. This only affects the score when at least two instruments participated, so single-instrument specialists are not penalized for being intentionally focused.
components.label: Instrument consensus

components.detail: Checks whether entry signals are sparse enough to behave like deliberate opportunities instead of bar-to-bar saturation. It is intentionally a signal hygiene input, separate from trade cadence, and only participates when signal behavior data exists.
components.label: Entry spacing hygiene

components.detail: Penalizes exits whose stop distance is so small that spread, slippage, and commission consume a large fraction of each R. Costs are still applied arithmetically in replay; this makes the strategy-shape risk visible to Score Lab.
components.label: Execution friction

components.detail: Penalizes strategies that keep trades open for too long. Long holds create swap exposure, weekend risk, unavailable margin, and a poor operator experience even when final realized R looks good.
components.formula: geomean(exp(-avg_holding_hours / 72), exp(-p90_holding_hours / 168), exp(-max_holding_hours / 720)); floored at 3% when present
components.label: Hold burden

components.detail: Penalizes profiles that spend long stretches inactive, flat, or unable to make new equity highs. This catches the visual failure mode where most of the chart is dead and the useful behavior appears only in a narrow regime.
components.formula: geomean(exp(-longest_no_new_high_days / 240), exp(-ending_no_new_high_days / 180), exp(-inactive window edges / 180), active_span_ratio band); floored at 3% when present
components.label: Dormancy

axes.detail: Operational and evidence-window sanity checks. This axis asks whether the profile looks usable as a repeatable trading object, not just impressive in a short or narrow test.
axes.formula: weighted geomean: cadence 1.0x, longevity 1.35x, consensus 1.0x when 2+ instruments, entry spacing 1.25x, execution friction 1.35x, hold burden 1.75x, dormancy 1.55x
axes.label: Viability
